message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 12
Recent Green phase: Implemented the cursed surcharge. Added optional material/enchantment/cursed fields to the Item interface and a CURSE_SURCHARGE=0.5 constant. Restructured premium calculation: item-specific surcharges (first-insurance 0.1, curse 0.5) are now additive percentages of an item's base premium. New helpers: itemSurchargeRate(item) (returns 0.1 + (cursed?0.5:0)), mainItemPremium(item) (base × (1+rate)), componentGroupPremium(count, perComponent) (component block base × 1.1). The old itemsBasePremium was renamed/reworked to itemsPremium which sums main-item premiums and component-group premiums. quotePremium now just adds the fee and rounds up.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all 12 tests green. Evaluate naming first (note itemSurchargeRate currently only includes first-insurance + curse; high-enchantment will be added later — keep the name general enough), then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass before/after. Watch for any duplication between mainItemPremium and componentGroupPremium (both multiply a base by a surcharge factor). Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the current implementation and test files to understand what I'm working with.
Let me verify all tests are currently green before refactoring.

input.description: Run tests to verify green baseline

content.text:
All 12 tests pass. Now let me work through the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Let me evaluate the names, paying attention to the hint about `mainItemPremium` vs `componentGroupPremium` duplication.

**Naming Evaluation:**

- `itemSurchargeRate(item)` — Returns `0.1 + (cursed?0.5:0)`. The name is appropriately general (high-enchantment will join later). **Keep.**
- `mainItemPremium(item)` — `base × (1 + surchargeRate)`. Clear. **Keep.**
- `componentGroupPremium(count, perComponent)` — `componentBasePremium × (1 + 0.1)`. Clear. **Keep.**
- `componentBasePremium`, `countByType`, `isComponent`, `isMainItem`, `sum`, `roundPremiumUp` — all reveal intent well. **Keep.**

Naming is sound. The function `itemSurchargeRate` correctly captures current intent and is general enough for the future high-enchantment surcharge.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Let me focus on the duplication the prompt flagged: `mainItemPremium` and `componentGroupPremium`. Both compute `base × (1 + surchargeRate)`.

```typescript
const mainItemPremium = (item) =>
  BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] * (1 + itemSurchargeRate(item));

const componentGroupPremium = (count, perComponent) =>
  componentBasePremium(count, perComponent) * (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE);
```

There's a shared concept: **apply a surcharge rate to a base amount** → `base × (1 + rate)`. This is conceptual duplication (Rule 3). For a main item the rate is `itemSurchargeRate(item)`; for a component group the rate is just `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE`. Both reduce to: a base premium gets the first-insurance surcharge, and additionally a main item may get a curse surcharge.

**Mass of the two functions + the multiplication pattern (current):**

`mainItemPremium`:
- Bindings: `item`, `item.type` (property access × 2 via `[item.type]` and inside surchargeRate), `BASE_PREMIUM` ≈ 3
- Invocations: `itemSurchargeRate()` = 1 → 2
- Constants: `1` = 1
- Operators (`*`, `+`): treated as invocations ≈ 2 → 4
- Approx subtotal: ~10

`componentGroupPremium`:
- Bindings: `count`, `perComponent` = 2
- Invocations: `componentBasePremium()` = 1 → 2
- Constants: `1` = 1
- Operators (`*`, `+`): ~2 → 4
- Approx subtotal: ~9

The `(1 + rate)` multiplication appears literally in two places — that's the duplication to extract.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** ✅ All 12 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Already strong. I'll preserve it.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** The `base × (1 + rate)` pattern is duplicated. Extract a small helper `applySurcharge(base, rate)` that names the concept. Then both call sites express their intent in terms of it.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** Extracting adds one element but removes conceptual duplication and centralizes the "surcharge applied to a base" rule — net win for maintainability without harming clarity.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll extract `applySurcharge(base, rate)` and route both premium functions through it. This also makes `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE` an explicit "rate" at the component call site, matching `itemSurchargeRate(item)` at the main-item call site — symmetric and intention-revealing.

content.text:
All 12 tests stay green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The `(1 + rate)` multiplication concept now lives in a single named place, `applySurcharge`. The two call sites no longer each spell out `* (1 + ...)`.

**Before** (the two premium functions):
- `mainItemPremium`: `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` (binding+access ~2) + `* (1 + itemSurchargeRate(item))` → constant `1` (1), `+` op (~1 invocation = 2), `*` op (~1 invocation = 2), call `itemSurchargeRate` (2). Subtotal ≈ 9.
- `componentGroupPremium`: call `componentBasePremium` (2) + `* (1 + FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE)` → constant `1` (1), two ops (~4), binding (1). Subtotal ≈ 8.
- Combined ≈ 17, with the `(1 + …) ×` arithmetic duplicated.

**After:**
- `applySurcharge`: bindings `base`, `rate` (2) + constant `1` (1) + two ops `+`,`*` (~4). Subtotal ≈ 7.
- `mainItemPremium`: `applySurcharge(...)` call (2), `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` (~2), `itemSurchargeRate(item)` call (2). Subtotal ≈ 6.
- `componentGroupPremium`: `applySurcharge(...)` call (2), `componentBasePremium(...)` call (2). Subtotal ≈ 4.
- Combined ≈ 17.

**Mass Change:** ≈ 17 → ≈ 17 (Δ ≈ 0). The total mass is essentially unchanged because extraction trades inline arithmetic for an invocation. The win here is **Rule 3 (no duplication)**: the surcharge-application formula now has a single representation, so a future change to how surcharges combine touches one site. The two call sites are now symmetric (each passes a base and a rate), which also strengthens **Rule 2 (reveals intent)** — `applySurcharge(componentBasePremium(...), FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE)` reads as a sentence.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Naming (evaluated first): All names already reveal intent; `itemSurchargeRate` confirmed general enough for the upcoming high-enchantment surcharge. No renames needed.
- ✅ Rule 3 (No Duplication): Extracted `applySurcharge(base, rate)` to remove the conceptual `base × (1 + rate)` duplication shared by `mainItemPremium` and `componentGroupPremium`.
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Both call sites now express the same idea symmetrically (a base premium plus a surcharge rate), reading as prose.
- ➖ APP mass: ≈ 17 → ≈ 17 (unchanged). Clarity/DRY were the goal, not mass reduction — and Rule 2/3 take priority over APP.

I considered going further (e.g., folding the curse logic into a per-rate table or unifying main vs component pricing entirely), but that would couple two distinct domain concepts (per-item surcharges vs per-component-group pricing) and obscure intent ahead of the high-enchantment, curse-scope, and loyalty tests still on the list — a Rule 2 regression. Holding off until those tests force the shape.

## Step 7: Report

🔄 Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `applySurcharge(base, rate)` helper, removing the duplicated `base × (1 + rate)` surcharge formula from `mainItemPremium` and `componentGroupPremium` and making both call sites symmetric and intention-revealing.
- **Mass Change:** ≈ 17 → ≈ 17 (Δ ≈ 0; the change targets duplication/clarity, which outrank APP mass).
- **Tests:** All 12 passing ✅

File changed: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
